title: Thinking in React
tagline: The official mental model for breaking UI into a component tree.

content.description: Before you write a single line of code, learn to see the interface as a tree of components. This is the mental shift that separates React beginners from React thinkers.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique walks you through the five-step process React engineers use to decompose any UI — from a simple mockup to a fully interactive component tree. By the end, you'll stop guessing where to put state and start reasoning about it.

title: useState: Managing Component State
tagline: Learn how to add and update local state inside your React components.

content.description: State is what makes a React component alive. Without it, your UI is just a static painting. With it, your components can remember, react, and respond.

title: useEffect In Depth

content.description: useEffect is the most misunderstood hook in React. Most developers use it — few truly understand it. This scroll will change that.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique walks you through the mental model of synchronization — not lifecycle. You'll learn how the dependency array really works, when to use cleanup functions, and how to stop writing effects that fire twice, loop infinitely, or fetch stale data.

content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers table structure, thead, tbody, tfoot, colspan, rowspan, caption, and accessibility considerations. You'll build a data table that is both semantic and screen-reader friendly.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Use the right tool for the right data.

title: HTML Media Elements
tagline: Images, video, audio, and canvas — native media in the browser.

content.description: The web is a rich media platform. HTML gives you native elements for images, video, and audio — no plugin required.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers img (with srcset and sizes), video, audio, figure, figcaption, and the picture element for responsive images. You'll optimize media for performance and accessibility.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Rich media, handled responsibly.

title: Web Performance & HTML
tagline: Load faster with defer, async, preload, and lazy loading.

content.description: Performance begins in the HTML. The order of your tags, the attributes on your scripts, and the way you load resources all affect how fast your page appears.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers script defer and async, link preload and prefetch, image lazy loading, and critical rendering path optimization. You'll measure and improve a real page's load performance.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. Speed is not a feature — it is respect for your user's time.

title: HTML Custom Data Attributes
tagline: Store extra information in your HTML with data-* attributes.

content.whatYouLearn: You'll learn how to set, read, and use data attributes with JavaScript's dataset API. You'll see practical examples in filtering, animations, and component state.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Hidden in plain sight — data that speaks to JavaScript.

title: HTML Canvas Basics
tagline: Draw graphics, charts, and animations with the HTML Canvas API.

content.description: Canvas is where HTML meets low-level graphics programming. It powers games, data visualizations, and real-time graphics in the browser.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers the 2D rendering context, drawing shapes and paths, images, transformations, and animation with requestAnimationFrame. You'll build a simple interactive drawing app.
content.quote: Sensei: an advanced technique. The blank canvas is not emptiness — it is infinite possibility.

title: TypeScript in 5 Minutes
tagline: Your first TypeScript program — from zero to typed in minutes.

content.description: TypeScript starts with JavaScript and adds types. The official 5-minute introduction is the fastest way to understand what you're getting into.
content.whatYouLearn: You'll write your first TypeScript program, understand the basic type annotations, and see how TypeScript catches errors that JavaScript would miss. The starting point for every TypeScript journey.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. A typed mind is a clear mind.

title: TypeScript Basic Types
tagline: string, number, boolean, array, tuple, and more.

content.description: Types are TypeScript's vocabulary. Learning the basic types is the first step to writing code that is self-documenting and error-resistant.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers primitive types, arrays, tuples, enums, any, unknown, void, and never. You'll understand type inference and when to annotate explicitly versus let TypeScript figure it out.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Name your types — name your intentions.
content.pathForward: Open the source link to begin training. When you finish, return here and save the scroll — the way of the Ronin is to keep your collection tight, intentional, and revisitable.

title: TypeScript Interfaces & Types
tagline: Define the shape of your objects with interfaces and type aliases.

content.description: Interfaces and type aliases are TypeScript's tools for describing the shape of objects. Choosing between them — and knowing their differences — is a core TypeScript skill.
content.whatYouLearn: You'll learn how to define interfaces, extend them, use type aliases, and understand the key differences between interface and type. You'll type real-world data structures like API responses and component props.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. A contract written in types is a contract that machines can verify.
content.pathForward: Open the source link to begin training. When you finish, return here and save the scroll — the way of the Ronin is to keep your collection tight, intentional, and revisitable.

title: TypeScript Generics
tagline: Write flexible, reusable code that works with any type.

content.description: Generics are TypeScript's way of writing code that is both flexible and type-safe. They are the foundation of reusable, well-typed libraries.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers generic functions, generic interfaces, generic constraints, and utility types like Partial, Required, Pick, and Omit. You'll write your first generic data fetching hook.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. Generics are the art of writing code for all types without sacrificing safety for any.
content.pathForward: Open the source link to begin training. When you finish, return here and save the scroll — the way of the Ronin is to keep your collection tight, intentional, and revisitable.

title: TypeScript with React
tagline: Type your components, props, hooks, and events.

content.description: React and TypeScript are a natural pair. Typed props catch bugs at compile time, typed hooks prevent runtime errors, and typed events make event handling predictable.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers typing component props, useState, useRef, useReducer, event handlers, and context. You'll convert a JavaScript React component to TypeScript step by step.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. Types are the documentation that never goes out of date.
content.pathForward: Open the source link to begin training. When you finish, return here and save the scroll — the way of the Ronin is to keep your collection tight, intentional, and revisitable.

title: TypeScript Utility Types
tagline: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record — TypeScript's built-in helpers.

content.description: TypeScript ships with a powerful set of utility types that transform existing types into new ones. They eliminate boilerplate and make your type system more expressive.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers Partial, Required, Readonly, Pick, Omit, Record, Exclude, Extract, NonNullable, and ReturnType. You'll use them to type real-world scenarios like form state and API responses.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. The tools that come with the dojo are there for a reason — learn them.

title: TypeScript Enums & Literal Types
tagline: Constrain values to a fixed set with enums and union literals.

content.description: When a value can only be one of a few options, TypeScript gives you the tools to enforce that constraint at compile time — eliminating an entire class of runtime errors.
content.whatYouLearn: You'll learn string enums, numeric enums, const enums, and union literal types. You'll understand when to use each and the tradeoffs between them.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Constrained choices lead to predictable outcomes.
content.pathForward: Open the source link to begin training. When you finish, return here and save the scroll — the way of the Ronin is to keep your collection tight, intentional, and revisitable.

title: TypeScript Type Narrowing
tagline: Help TypeScript understand your types at runtime with guards.

content.description: TypeScript can't always know the exact type of a value. Type narrowing is how you guide it — and unlock more precise type information in different code branches.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers typeof guards, instanceof checks, in operator narrowing, discriminated unions, and user-defined type guards. You'll handle API responses and union types with confidence.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. Narrowing is the art of knowing exactly what you're dealing with.
content.pathForward: Open the source link to begin training. When you finish, return here and save the scroll — the way of the Ronin is to keep your collection tight, intentional, and revisitable.

title: Advanced TypeScript Patterns
tagline: Conditional types, mapped types, and template literal types.

content.description: Advanced TypeScript is a programming language within a programming language. Mastering it gives you the power to build type-safe abstractions that would be impossible otherwise.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers conditional types, infer keyword, mapped types, template literal types, and recursive types. You'll build advanced utility types from scratch.
content.quote: Sensei: an advanced technique. At this level, types become logic. Master the logic.

title: TypeScript Configuration (tsconfig)

content.description: tsconfig.json is the control panel of your TypeScript project. Understanding its options lets you configure TypeScript exactly as strict or lenient as your project needs.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers the most important tsconfig options — strict, target, module, paths, baseUrl, and lib. You'll understand what strict mode enables and why you should always use it.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. A well-configured tool is a productive tool.

title: Git Fundamentals
tagline: init, add, commit, push — your first Git workflow.

content.description: Git is the time machine of software development. Every professional developer uses it — and every beginner should learn it before writing their first line of production code.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers the core Git workflow — init, add, commit, status, log, and push. You'll understand the working directory, staging area, and repository — and why each step exists.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Every commit is a checkpoint. Save often — regret never.

title: Git Branching Strategy

content.description: Branches are what make Git truly powerful. They let you work on features in isolation, experiment without fear, and collaborate without conflict.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers branch creation, switching, merging, and deletion. You'll learn the feature branch workflow — the standard used by most professional development teams.
content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. Branch freely — merge intentionally.

title: Git Merge vs Rebase
tagline: Understand when to merge and when to rebase — and why it matters.

content.description: Merge and rebase both integrate changes — but they tell different stories. Understanding the difference makes you a more intentional collaborator.

content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. History is not just what happened — it is what you choose to record.

title: Git Commit Message Conventions
tagline: Write commit messages that communicate intent, not just action.

content.description: A good commit message is a gift to your future self. It tells the story of why a change was made — not just what changed.

title: GitHub Pull Requests & Code Review
tagline: The collaborative workflow that powers open source and team development.

title: Git Stash & Cherry-Pick
tagline: Save work in progress and apply specific commits across branches.

content.description: Sometimes you need to switch context without committing unfinished work. Sometimes you need just one commit from another branch. Git stash and cherry-pick are your tools.
content.whatYouLearn: This technique covers git stash, stash list, stash pop, stash apply, and git cherry-pick. You'll handle the common scenario of needing to pull changes while you have uncommitted work.
content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. The flexible warrior adapts — the flexible developer stashes.

title: Git Reset, Revert & Restore
tagline: Undo mistakes in Git without fear.

content.description: Everyone makes mistakes in Git. The difference between a junior and a senior developer is knowing how to undo them cleanly — and which undo command to use.

content.quote: Senpai: an intermediate technique. The ability to undo is not weakness — it is wisdom.

title: .gitignore Best Practices
tagline: Keep sensitive files and build artifacts out of your repository.

content.quote: Kohai: a beginner technique. What you exclude is as important as what you include.
